exemplar.title: To what extent does Tenessee Williams present Blanche DuBoisâs identity and character development through the depiction of her mental health in the 1947 play âStreetcar Named Desireâ?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does Tenessee Williams present Blanche DuBoisâs identity and character development through the depiction of her mental health in the 1947 play âStreetcar Named Desireâ?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: Satisfactory familiarity with key events and thematic concerns of the play
Use of several relevant direct quotations to frame the argument
weaknesses: Repeated misattribution of Tennessee Williamsâs text to Fugard
Mislabeling Blanche as the antagonist undermines interpretative accuracy
Unsubstantiated clinical assertions (e.g. borderline personality disorder) lacking textual support
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The response demonstrates a solid, but inconsistent, knowledge of the playâs events and thematic implications. References are generally relevant yet marred by sloppy attributions and occasional simplifications. These factors place the work firmly in band 3 for Knowledge, Understanding and Interpretation.

strengths: Insightful discussion of the naked light bulb motif linking exposure to Blancheâs psyche
Appropriate analysis of symbolism (paper lantern, moon) moving beyond mere description
weaknesses: Analysis sometimes drifts into unsupported psychological speculation
Limited close reading of Williamsâs diction and stage directions to deepen argument
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While much of the analysis is descriptive, there are moments of genuine insightâparticularly in the treatment of light symbolismâthat rise to band 4. The evaluation is appropriate and occasionally persuasive, warranting a score of 4 in Analysis and Evaluation.

strengths: Clear line of inquiry stated in the introduction and consistently revisited
Logical paragraph progression (instability â escapism â self-image â collapse) creating cohesion
weaknesses: Minor digressions (clinical labels) momentarily disrupt focus
Quotations are sometimes awkwardly integrated, interrupting sentence flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay is well organized and mostly cohesive, with a fully developed line of inquiry and purposeful progression of ideas. Occasional unwieldy sentences and integration issues prevent the top band, but overall performance aligns with band 4.

strengths: Language is generally clear and employs appropriate academic vocabulary
Register remains suitably formal for a literary-analysis essay
weaknesses: Frequent spelling and typographical errors (e.g., âTenesseeâ)
Run-on sentences and awkward phrasing impede smooth reading
Inconsistent citation formatting and occasional subjectâverb agreement errors
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The candidateâs language use is adequate and comprehensible, with lapses in accuracy and precision. The register is appropriate but not consistently polished, placing the work in band 3 for Language.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Quotations are embedded within the prose (ââ¦she yells, âI donât want realism, I want magic!â when Mitchâ¦â) so that sentences generally read smoothly. Nevertheless, some citations interrupt syntax or appear as stand-alone lines.  
Because integration is **inconsistent but present more often than not**, band 3ââsometimes integratedââis the best fit.

The student demonstrates **satisfactory familiarity with the playâs events, chronology and thematic concerns**. Key plot moments (the paper lantern scene, Mitchâs confrontation, the poker game and the closing removal to the asylum) are correctly recalled and used to frame the discussion of mental health.  
However, there are **noticeable lapses and simplifications**: Blanche is repeatedly mis-labelled the âantagonistâ, Fugard is cited instead of Williams, and complex contextual ideas (e.g. âborderline personality disorderâ) are asserted without textual grounding. These inaccuracies limit the depth of understanding.  
Overall, knowledge and interpretation move beyond the superficial and do engage with implications, but the insight is inconsistent, placing the work solidly in the middle band.

The line of inquiry is announced clearly in the introduction and revisited in each body section, giving the essay a **coherent, mostly cohesive structure**. Paragraphs follow a logical progression (instability â escapism â self-image â collapse) and end with linking commentary, evidencing solid organisation.  
Minor digressions (e.g., extended clinical labels) and some unwieldy sentences affect flow, but the essay maintains reader orientation throughout. Development of the argument is adequate and usually purposeful, fulfilling the descriptor for âwell organised and mostly cohesiveâ in band 4.

The essay includes **several direct quotations** (âYoung man!â, âI canât stand a naked light bulbâ, âI donât want realism â I want magicâ) which are relevant to the stated line of inquiry and usually linked to the argument about fantasy and self-image.  
Yet the student sometimes cites secondary paraphrases rather than the original text and incorrectly attributes all quotations to âFugardâ, showing **sloppy bibliographic control**. Reference to critical sources is minimal and largely relegated to the Works Cited.  
Hence, references are generally useful and mostly supportive, but selection precision and attribution weaknesses prevent the work reaching the âeffective/well-chosenâ standard of the higher bands.

The tone remains **formally academic** and objective, suitable for literary analysis. Although a few conversational asides appear (âThis warrants sending Blanche awayâ¦â), overall register aligns with expectations for an HL essay.  
Given this generally appropriate yet not consistently polished application of style, band 3 is warranted.

Language is **largely clear and comprehensible**, with appropriate academic vocabulary. Errors in spelling (âTenesseeâ), awkward phrasing and occasional run-on sentences occur but seldom impede meaning.  
Accuracy is therefore adequate rather than exemplary, meeting the descriptor for band 3 where clarity is maintained despite lapses.

The student moves beyond narration by examining **symbolism (paper lantern, moon), motif (light/shadow) and dialogue** to connect Blancheâs behaviour with mental decline. This reflects **appropriate if not penetrating analysis**.  
Much commentary, however, drifts into description (âThis juxtaposes the reality sheâs facingâ¦â) or psychological speculation unsupported by close stylistic study. Analytical moments are present but not sustained, and evaluation of Williamsâs craft is more asserted than argued.  
Consequently, the response meets the descriptors for band 3: analysis is generally appropriate, though rarely insightful or convincing.

annotations.comment: Consider how the pacing and placement of each symbol contribute structurally to the playâs progression toward Blancheâs collapse.
The discussion of the naked light bulb vividly captures Blancheâs fear of exposure, effectively linking motif to character.
The line of inquiry contains a spelling error in âTenessee Williams,â which should read âTennessee Williams.â
The title effectively frames the essayâs academic purpose, giving a clear indication of level and focus.
The author is misattributed as âFugardâ instead of Tennessee Williams, undermining credibility in literary context.
Labeling Blanche as the âantagonistâ misrepresents her role as the playâs central protagonist and weakens your interpretation.
Asserting that Blanche has borderline personality disorder requires textual evidence; integrate dialogue or stage directions that illustrate her symptoms.
The introduction successfully situates the play within dramatic naturalism, providing useful context for the argumentâs development.
Consider narrowing the question by specifying which scenes or symbols you will analyze to make the inquiry more focused and manageable.
The word count exceeds the HL limit of 1,500 words by about 50 words; consider pruning repetitions to adhere to guidelines.
The quotation âYoung man!â¦â is incorrectly cited to Fugard; ensure all attributions correctly name Williams for scholarly accuracy.
Several sentences in this paragraph exceed 50 words, causing reader fatigue; break them into shorter, focused statements.
The analysis of the âpaper moonâ lyrics identifies its symbolic value but would benefit from closer examination of Williamsâs specific diction.
Again attributing quotations to Fugard weakens source credibility; maintain consistency and accuracy in citations.
Vary sentence openings to avoid repetitive structure and maintain reader engagement in analysis paragraphs.
Incorporate stage directionsâe.g., dimming or blackout cuesâto reinforce how lighting choices shape Blancheâs psychology.
To deepen evaluation, quote stage instructions around the poker scene to show how Williams directs audience focus onto Blanche.
Typo: âthe subtlest of blemishesâ should read âthe subtlest blemishesâ for clarity and grammatical correctness.
The concluding paragraph reaffirms your thesis but introduces no fresh insightâconsider adding a broader implication to leave a stronger final impression.
The linkage between the paper lantern and the moon symbol is well articulated, illustrating the fragility of Blancheâs illusions.
Subjectâverb disagreement: âits implications playsâ should be âits implications play.â
The isolated fragment âstruggles led to impending mental deteriorationâ lacks context and should be integrated into a full sentence.
Awkward phrasing in âthwarted her into experiencingâ should be revised for smoother readability.
The assertion that Blanche âwarrants sending away for psychological treatmentâ needs supporting evidence from dialogue or stage action.
The LitCharts entry lacks author, publication date, and proper formattingârevisit MLA/APA conventions for web sources.
The PMT source includes stray math symbols (â\Box Ïâ) and inconsistent URL formatting; clean up for clarity and professionalism.
Citations vary in style between entries; standardize punctuation, italics, and dates to maintain consistent bibliographic presentation.
Include full URL or DOI and access date for the Context PDF to enhance traceability of your secondary sources.
The primary text entry omits publisher location and page range; add these details for a complete citation.
